seo.title: Claude Code vs Codex vs GitHub Copilot
seo.description: Compare three coding agents by workflow, execution environment, repository integration, automation, and control for shipping AI-coded web apps.

content.intro: Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, and GitHub Copilot can all inspect repositories, edit multiple files, and run development commands, but they enter a team's workflow from different directions. This comparison focuses on durable differences: where the agent runs, how it receives project context, how naturally it fits GitHub, and how much control a developer keeps during execution.

options.name: Claude Code
options.best_for: Developers who want an interactive terminal agent with strong repository exploration and explicit command approval controls.

options.name: OpenAI Codex
options.tagline: Coding agent available across local and cloud workflows
options.best_for: Teams that want to move between local collaboration and delegated coding tasks in isolated environments.

options.name: GitHub Copilot
options.tagline: AI coding assistance embedded across GitHub and editors
options.best_for: GitHub-centric teams that want completion, chat, review, and coding-agent workflows under one product.

criteria.name: Primary interface
criteria.description: The workflow each product treats as its natural home.

values.OpenAI Codex: CLI, editor, app, and delegated cloud tasks
values.GitHub Copilot: IDE, CLI, GitHub website, pull requests, and agents

criteria.name: Task execution
criteria.description: How the agent performs multi-step repository work.

values.Claude Code: Works in the local checkout and invokes approved tools and commands
values.OpenAI Codex: Can work locally or execute delegated tasks in isolated cloud environments
values.GitHub Copilot: Offers local agent workflows and GitHub-hosted coding-agent tasks

criteria.name: GitHub workflow
criteria.description: How directly the product participates in issues, pull requests, and review.

values.Claude Code: Can use GitHub through its CLI and integrations, but is not tied to GitHub
values.OpenAI Codex: Can produce changes for review and connect delegated work to repositories
values.GitHub Copilot: Deep native integration with repositories, pull requests, issues, and code review
Uses repository custom instructions, agent files, and skills

criteria.name: Human control
criteria.description: The practical model for approving edits and commands.

values.Claude Code: Granular permission modes and interactive approvals
values.OpenAI Codex: Sandbox and approval policies vary by surface and task
values.GitHub Copilot: Controls vary across IDE agent mode, CLI, and cloud agent

criteria.name: Editor assistance
criteria.description: Support for everyday inline coding while a developer types.

values.Claude Code: Agent-focused; IDE integrations complement the terminal
values.OpenAI Codex: Agent-focused editor integration rather than a universal completion layer
values.GitHub Copilot: Mature inline completions plus chat and agent modes

criteria.name: Automation fit
criteria.description: Suitability for scripts, repeatable jobs, and unattended tasks.

values.Claude Code: Print mode and SDK support scripted execution
values.OpenAI Codex: Supports delegated tasks, automation, and SDK-driven workflows
values.GitHub Copilot: Automations and GitHub events fit repository-centered jobs

criteria.name: Best adoption path
criteria.description: The least disruptive way to introduce the product.

values.Claude Code: Start with one repository and a reviewed CLAUDE.md
values.OpenAI Codex: Start locally, then delegate bounded tasks once checks are reliable
values.GitHub Copilot: Enable IDE assistance first, then add code review and coding agent

verdict.summary: Choose based on workflow topology, not benchmark anecdotes. Claude Code is a strong terminal collaborator, Codex spans local and delegated execution, and GitHub Copilot has the broadest native GitHub and inline-assistance footprint. All three still require good repository instructions, deterministic tests, and human review.

use_cases.reason: Its interactive terminal workflow and explicit permission model make iterative repository work easy to supervise.
Its local-to-cloud workflow is designed for handing off tasks while retaining review gates.
It connects editor assistance, pull requests, code review, and repository agents inside the existing GitHub workflow.
